requirements.english: TOEFL/IELTS no longer required for Chemistry applicants; English proficiency is assessed through faculty interviews.
requirements.admission_model_note: Admissions are decided by a faculty committee and students are admitted to the department rather than to a specific advisor. PhD students typically choose a research group by the end of the first term rather than through formal lab rotations.

funding.note: PhD students receive full support (stipend plus tuition) through research assistantships, teaching assistantships, and fellowships.

requirements.contact_note: Applicants are admitted to the department, not to individual advisors, so contacting faculty in advance is not required.

programs.data_currency: Researched 2026-07-05 from official pages — verify before applying.

faculty.name: Theodor Agapie
faculty.title: John Stauffer Professor of Chemistry; Executive Officer of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Agapie's group designs multimetallic complexes to understand and drive small-molecule activation. Recent work builds synthetic models of the oxygen-evolving Mn4Ca cluster of photosystem II to probe how redox-inactive metals tune water oxidation, and develops molecular and electrode-supported catalysts for CO2 and CO reduction to multicarbon products. The group also studies secondary-sphere and cation effects in electrocatalysis and organometallic intermediates relevant to nitrogen and hydrocarbon chemistry, bridging bioinorganic modeling with practical energy conversion.

faculty.name: Garnet K. Chan
faculty.title: Bren Professor of Chemistry; Director of the Rudolph A. Marcus Center for Theoretical Chemistry
faculty.summary: Chan develops methods to simulate the quantum mechanics of complex molecules and materials. His group advances the density matrix renormalization group, tensor networks, and quantum embedding theories to treat strongly correlated electrons in transition-metal clusters, solids, and catalysts. Recent efforts target near-exact benchmarks for challenging systems, ab initio simulation of superconductors and correlated materials, and algorithms for quantum computers. The work combines new theory with large-scale computation to make predictive electronic-structure calculations feasible for previously intractable problems.

faculty.name: Gregory C. Fu
faculty.title: Norman Chandler Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Fu develops enantioselective catalytic reactions, particularly transition-metal and photoinduced methods that forge carbon-carbon and carbon-heteroatom bonds via radical intermediates. His group is known for nickel-catalyzed asymmetric cross-couplings of alkyl electrophiles and for photoinduced, copper-catalyzed couplings of amines, thiols, and other nucleophiles. Recent work expands enantioconvergent radical reactions, mechanistic understanding of how chiral catalysts control open-shell intermediates, and new bond constructions that are difficult by classical two-electron pathways, broadening access to stereodefined building blocks.

faculty.name: Sarah E. Reisman
faculty.title: Bren Professor of Chemistry; Norman Davidson Leadership Chair, Division of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
faculty.summary: Reisman's group targets the total synthesis of structurally complex, bioactive natural products and uses these campaigns to inspire new catalytic methods. Recent work features nickel- and cobalt-catalyzed reductive cross-couplings, asymmetric bond-forming reactions, and electrochemically driven transformations that streamline access to polycyclic terpenoids and alkaloids. The group emphasizes strategies that build molecular complexity efficiently and stereoselectively, and it studies reaction mechanisms to render couplings enantioselective, connecting fundamental methodology development with the synthesis of challenging targets.

faculty.name: Brian M. Stoltz
faculty.title: Victor and Elizabeth Atkins Professor of Chemistry; Investigator, Heritage Medical Research Institute
faculty.summary: Stoltz pursues the total synthesis of complex natural products alongside the development of catalytic asymmetric methods, notably palladium-catalyzed enantioselective allylic alkylations that construct quaternary stereocenters. Recent campaigns deliver intricate alkaloid and terpenoid targets and translate synthetic insights into scalable routes and medicinal applications. The group also explores new catalytic C-C bond formations, C-H functionalization, and reaction discovery, using demanding targets to test and refine methodology while pursuing molecules of biological and therapeutic interest.

faculty.name: Jonas C. Peters
faculty.title: Bren Professor of Chemistry; Director, Resnick Sustainability Institute
faculty.summary: Peters designs transition-metal complexes and electrocatalysts for challenging small-molecule transformations, especially catalytic nitrogen-to-ammonia conversion under mild conditions. His group elucidates mechanisms of Fe- and Mo-mediated N2 reduction, develops metal complexes that manage proton-coupled electron transfer, and applies these principles to CO2 reduction and other energy-relevant reactions. Recent work also spans light-driven catalysis and merging molecular catalysis with electrode interfaces, aiming to convert abundant feedstocks into fuels and fertilizers sustainably.

faculty.name: Kimberly A. See
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: See's group studies the chemistry underlying next-generation energy-storage materials, focusing on multivalent-ion (such as magnesium and calcium) and alternative battery systems beyond lithium. Combining synthesis, spectroscopy, and operando characterization, the group probes ion transport, electrode-electrolyte interfaces, and reaction mechanisms that govern capacity and reversibility. Recent work examines conversion cathodes, sulfur redox, and speciation in nonaqueous electrolytes to identify design rules for safer, more abundant, and higher-energy batteries, connecting fundamental solid-state and electrochemistry to practical storage.

faculty.name: Maxwell J. Robb
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Robb develops mechanophores, molecules that undergo productive chemical change in response to mechanical force, and integrates them into polymers to create materials that sense and respond to stress. Recent work designs mechanophores that release small molecules, change color, or trigger cascade reactions when a material is stretched or damaged, and elucidates how molecular structure controls force reactivity. The group aims to harness mechanical energy for constructive chemistry, enabling self-reporting, damage-responsive, and remotely activated functional polymer materials.

faculty.name: Ryan G. Hadt
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Hadt combines advanced spectroscopy, electronic-structure theory, and synthesis to understand how the geometric and electronic structure of transition-metal complexes governs their function. Recent work targets molecular qubits and quantum information science, establishing design principles that lengthen spin coherence times in metal complexes, and probes excited-state and spin dynamics relevant to catalysis and magnetism. By connecting spectroscopic observables to structure, the group develops predictive frameworks for tuning electron transfer, reactivity, and quantum coherence in inorganic systems.

faculty.name: Scott K. Cushing
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Cushing develops table-top ultrafast X-ray and attosecond spectroscopies to watch electrons and energy move in materials and molecules on their natural timescales. His group builds high-harmonic and entangled-photon sources to probe charge-transfer, catalytic, and semiconductor dynamics with element specificity, and explores quantum-light spectroscopy for enhanced sensing. Recent work tracks carrier and structural dynamics in photocatalysts and quantum materials, aiming to connect femtosecond-to-attosecond electronic motion with function in energy-conversion and information technologies.

faculty.name: Hosea M. Nelson
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Nelson's group develops next-generation tools for organic chemistry, pairing new reaction discovery with structural methods. A signature effort adapts microcrystal electron diffraction (microED) into a routine, 'small-molecule cryo-EM' technique for rapidly determining structures of complex molecules from nanogram samples. The lab also studies reactive silylium and carbocation intermediates to enable C-H functionalization and catalysis, and applies these methods to the efficient total synthesis of bioactive natural products and potential therapeutics.

faculty.name: Harry B. Gray
faculty.title: Arnold O. Beckman Professor of Chemistry; Founding Director, Beckman Institute
faculty.summary: Gray's interdisciplinary program centers on electron-transfer chemistry spanning inorganic chemistry, biochemistry and biophysics. His group probes long-range electron tunneling in metalloproteins, using engineered redox probes to map biological charge-transport pathways and protein dynamics. A parallel effort pursues solar-fuels catalysis, developing earth-abundant materials and molecular catalysts that use sunlight and water to generate hydrogen. Gray, who helped found biological inorganic chemistry, continues to advance artificial photosynthesis and remains active through the Beckman Institute and solar-energy initiatives.

faculty.name: Nathan S. Lewis
faculty.title: George L. Argyros Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Lewis's Energy and Electrochemistry Laboratory studies the chemistry of semiconductor and nanostructured interfaces for renewable energy. The group develops solar-fuels systems that split water into hydrogen using sunlight, engineering silicon microwire arrays, protective coatings and earth-abundant electrocatalysts for efficient, stable photoelectrochemical cells. Related work targets artificial photosynthesis architectures and molecular-recognition-based chemical sensor arrays ('electronic noses'). Lewis has been a longtime leader in solar-energy conversion and the global-scale energy challenge.

faculty.name: Mitchio Okumura
faculty.title: Professor of Chemical Physics
faculty.summary: Okumura's group applies laser spectroscopy, kinetics and reaction dynamics to problems in atmospheric and combustion chemistry. Using high-resolution and time-resolved techniques, they characterize short-lived free radicals and transient intermediates, such as Criegee intermediates and peroxy radicals, that govern tropospheric and stratospheric photochemistry. The lab measures reaction rates, product branching and isotope effects to build fundamental, molecular-level understanding of processes controlling air quality and climate-relevant chemistry.

faculty.name: Sandeep Sharma
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Sharma, who moved to Caltech in 2025, develops advanced algorithms for electronic-structure theory to simulate strongly correlated molecules with high accuracy. His group pioneers methods including the density matrix renormalization group, semistochastic heat-bath configuration interaction and auxiliary-field and variational quantum Monte Carlo, combining physics, chemistry and computer science. These tools target challenging systems relevant to catalysis, transition-metal complexes and materials, and increasingly connect classical simulation with quantum-computing approaches to chemistry.

faculty.name: William A. Goddard III
faculty.title: Charles and Mary Ferkel Professor of Chemistry, Materials Science, and Applied Physics
faculty.summary: Goddard directs the Materials and Process Simulation Center, predicting properties of chemical, biological and materials systems directly from first principles. His group builds a hierarchy of methods spanning quantum mechanics, reactive force fields (ReaxFF), electron dynamics and multiscale molecular dynamics. Applications include heterogeneous and electro-catalysis, battery and fuel-cell materials, polymers, and structure-based design of GPCR-targeted drugs, translating fundamental theory into predictive design of new catalysts and materials.

faculty.name: Shasha Chong
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Chemistry; Ronald and JoAnne Willens Scholar
faculty.summary: Chong's lab works at the interface of chemistry, physics and biology to dissect how cells regulate transcription. Using single-molecule and super-resolution imaging in living cells, the group studies how transcription factors with intrinsically disordered regions form biomolecular condensates through phase separation to control gene expression. They connect these dynamic, multivalent interactions to normal cellular function and to disease states such as cancer, aiming to reveal and ultimately target aberrant condensate behavior.

faculty.name: Linda C. Hsieh-Wilson
faculty.title: Milton and Rosalind Chang Professor of Chemistry; Merkin Institute Professor
faculty.summary: Hsieh-Wilson's group develops chemical approaches to understand the roles of carbohydrates and protein glycosylation in the nervous system and disease. The lab devises efficient syntheses of defined oligosaccharides and glycan mimetics and builds tools to probe O-GlcNAc modification and chondroitin sulfate glycosaminoglycans. They have shown that specific sulfation patterns regulate neuronal signaling, plasticity and axon regeneration after injury, linking glycan structure to function in neurobiology and neurodegeneration.

faculty.name: Lu Wei
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry; Investigator, Heritage Medical Research Institute; Ronald and JoAnne Willens Scholar
faculty.summary: Wei's group works at the interface of optical spectroscopy, chemical biology and life science, creating vibrational imaging tools to visualize molecules in living systems. They advance stimulated Raman scattering microscopy and pre-resonance approaches to achieve super-multiplexed, high-sensitivity imaging with small bioorthogonal tags. Recent work enables label-free and super-resolution chemical imaging, quantifies disease hallmarks such as protein aggregation, and tracks subcellular metabolism, expanding the palette of colors available for imaging cellular chemistry.

faculty.name: Dennis A. Dougherty
faculty.title: George Grant Hoag Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Dougherty pioneered the study of cation-pi interactions and their role in protein structure, molecular recognition and enzyme catalysis. His group applies physical-organic principles and unnatural amino acid mutagenesis to neuroreceptors and ion channels, including nicotinic acetylcholine, serotonin and dopamine receptors. By incorporating subtly modified amino acids, the lab maps the precise chemical interactions, such as cation-pi binding and backbone hydrogen bonds, that govern how drugs like nicotine bind and activate their targets.

faculty.name: Karthish Manthiram
faculty.title: Bren Professor of Chemical Engineering and Chemistry; William H. Hurt Scholar; Executive Officer for Chemical Engineering
faculty.summary: Manthiram's lab creates electrified molecular systems for sustainable chemical manufacturing. The group designs heterogeneous electrocatalysts that convert abundant inputs, nitrogen, carbon dioxide and water, into fuels and chemicals using renewable electricity, and develops electrochemical routes for organic synthesis and ammonia production. A newer direction electrifies metabolism in living cells by controlling transmembrane charge transfer and cellular redox balance. Together this work advances distributed, decarbonized approaches to producing molecules on demand.

faculty.name: Johannes Morstein
faculty.title: Assistant Professor of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
faculty.summary: Morstein, who joined Caltech in 2025, leads a chemical biology group that uses chemistry to study, target and reprogram the human lipidome. Integrating organic synthesis with molecular and cell biology, high-throughput screening, lipidomics and imaging, the lab builds photoswitchable lipids and small molecules for photopharmacology, enabling light control of biological processes. Examples include photoswitchable ceramides that optically control apoptosis, tools for phosphatidic acid signaling, and light-gated inhibitors of membrane transporters.

requirements.english: TOEFL iBT minimum 90 or IELTS minimum 7.0; waived with one year of full-time study at a recognized U.S. (or English-medium) institution.
requirements.admission_model_note: Students are admitted to the department by a faculty committee and select a research advisor during the first semester after meeting with groups; the Chemistry program does not use formal lab rotations.

funding.note: All admitted PhD students receive full support (stipend, tuition, and fees) through a mix of fellowships, teaching assistantships, and research assistantships for the duration of study, contingent on good progress.

requirements.contact_note: The department states contacting faculty is not required, though applicants may list faculty of interest on the application.

programs.data_currency: Researched 2026-07-05 from official pages — verify before applying.

faculty.name: F. Dean Toste
faculty.title: Gerald E. K. Branch Distinguished Professor of Chemistry; Department Chair
faculty.summary: Toste develops new catalytic reactions and strategies for enantioselective synthesis, pioneering homogeneous gold catalysis and chiral anion-directed asymmetric catalysis. Recent work exploits ion pairing and supramolecular hosts to control reactivity and selectivity, advances catalytic fluorination and C-H functionalization, and applies catalysis to problems in energy and plastics upcycling. Collaborating across disciplines, the group studies confined catalytic environments and machine-learning-guided reaction development, uniting mechanistic physical-organic insight with the invention of practical, selective transformations.

faculty.name: John F. Hartwig
faculty.title: Henry Rapoport Chair in Organic Chemistry
faculty.summary: Hartwig develops transition-metal-catalyzed reactions that form carbon-heteroatom and carbon-carbon bonds, including foundational work on C-N cross-coupling and catalytic, site-selective C-H functionalization of complex molecules. Recent research advances earth-abundant base-metal catalysis, undirected borylation and functionalization, and the merger of chemical catalysis with biological catalysis through artificial metalloenzymes containing non-native metals. The group combines detailed mechanistic study with methodology, targeting selective, practical transformations for pharmaceutical synthesis, feedstock conversion, and new catalyst design.

faculty.name: Thomas J. Maimone
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Maimone's group develops concise, scalable total syntheses of architecturally complex natural products, particularly heavily oxidized and rearranged terpenoids. By identifying strategic bond disconnections and inventing new oxidation, cyclization, and rearrangement methods, the group compresses lengthy routes and enables access to biologically active meroterpenoids, limonoids, and related targets. Recent work also mines plant and microbial metabolites for chemistry-driven insights and pursues fragment-coupling and C-H functionalization strategies that make demanding molecules practically available for study.

faculty.name: Martin Head-Gordon
faculty.title: Kenneth S. Pitzer Distinguished Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Head-Gordon develops electronic-structure theory and algorithms that make accurate quantum-chemical calculations practical for large molecules. His group is known for widely used density functionals (including the wB97 family), efficient coupled-cluster and perturbation methods, and energy-decomposition analyses that dissect intermolecular interactions and bonding. Recent work improves the accuracy-cost tradeoff of DFT, characterizes reaction mechanisms and open-shell species, and creates tools that interpret chemical bonding, supporting both fundamental theory and applications across catalysis and materials.

faculty.name: Peidong Yang
faculty.title: S.K. and Angela Chan Distinguished Professor of Energy; Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Yang's group synthesizes inorganic nanomaterials, including semiconductor nanowires, halide perovskites, and metal nanocrystals, and applies them to energy conversion and catalysis. A signature effort couples light-absorbing nanowires with microbes or molecular catalysts to build artificial-photosynthetic systems that convert CO2 and water into fuels and chemicals. Recent work explores nanocrystal superlattices, perovskite optoelectronics, and interfacial charge transfer for solar-to-chemical conversion, combining precise materials synthesis with mechanistic study to push efficiency and selectivity in sustainable energy technologies.

faculty.name: Daniel M. Neumark
faculty.title: Melvin Calvin Distinguished Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Neumark studies chemical dynamics using ultrafast lasers and molecular beams. His group pioneered anion photoelectron methods to characterize transition states and reactive intermediates, and now applies attosecond and femtosecond XUV spectroscopy to follow electron and nuclear motion in atoms, molecules, and liquids. Recent work probes charge and energy relaxation in liquid microjets, dynamics of clusters and nanodroplets, and attosecond charge migration, providing detailed, time-resolved pictures of fundamental processes that underpin photochemistry and energy flow.

faculty.name: T. Don Tilley
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Tilley's group explores synthetic and mechanistic inorganic and organometallic chemistry aimed at catalysis and materials. Longstanding interests include metal-silicon and metal-heteroatom multiple bonds, sigma-bond metathesis, and reactive intermediates that enable new bond activations. The group designs molecular precursors and single-site catalysts for polymerization, hydrocarbon functionalization, and energy-relevant reactions such as water oxidation, and prepares tailored nanomaterials from molecular building blocks, connecting fundamental coordination chemistry to practical catalytic and materials applications.

faculty.name: Ke Xu
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Xu develops advanced optical microscopy to visualize chemistry and structure in cells at the nanoscale and single-molecule level. His group created spectrally resolved single-molecule imaging and functional super-resolution methods that map local physicochemical properties such as polarity, viscosity, and charge inside cells. Recent work images membrane organization, the cytoskeleton, and cellular interfaces with molecular specificity, and extends single-molecule spectroscopy to reveal heterogeneity invisible to conventional techniques, bridging physical chemistry, imaging technology, and cell biology.

faculty.name: David T. Limmer
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Limmer develops statistical-mechanical theory and simulation to understand systems far from equilibrium. His group formulates methods for sampling rare events and computing dynamical fluctuations, and applies them to charge transfer at electrochemical interfaces, self-assembly, energy transport in nanomaterials, and driven, dissipative systems. Recent work clarifies how microscopic fluctuations control interfacial reactivity, exciton and carrier dynamics, and nonequilibrium self-organization, providing predictive frameworks that connect molecular-scale dynamics to emergent behavior in complex chemical and materials systems.

faculty.name: Kwabena Bediako
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Bediako investigates the chemistry and physics of two-dimensional and layered materials, engineering atomically thin interfaces to control electronic and electrochemical behavior. His group studies how twisting, stacking, and ion intercalation tune correlated and quantum states, and develops electrochemical methods to modify and probe these materials. Recent work combines synthesis, electrochemistry, and X-ray and transport measurements to reveal structure-property relationships in moire and layered systems, targeting new platforms for energy storage, catalysis, and quantum information at the chemistry-condensed-matter interface.

faculty.name: Richmond Sarpong
faculty.title: Maxine J. Elliott Endowed University Chair; Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Sarpong's group develops strategies and methods for synthesizing complex, biologically active natural products, especially architecturally intricate alkaloids and terpenoids. A central theme is inventing new C-C bond-forming and C-C bond-cleaving reactions, including strain-release and aromatization-driven approaches, that reorganize readily available scaffolds into challenging targets. The lab also formalizes retrosynthetic 'network analysis' logic and pyrolytic fragmentations, translating fundamental reaction discovery into shorter, more efficient, and more sustainable routes to medicinally relevant and stereochemically dense molecules.

faculty.name: Omar M. Yaghi
faculty.title: University Professor; James and Neeltje Tretter Endowed Chair in Chemistry
faculty.summary: Yaghi, a 2025 Nobel laureate in chemistry, pioneered reticular chemistry, the stitching of molecular building blocks into extended crystalline frameworks through strong bonds. His group created and developed metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs) and zeolitic imidazolate frameworks, materials with extraordinary porosity and tunable structure. Applications span hydrogen and methane storage, carbon dioxide capture, catalysis, and harvesting drinking water from desert air. The lab continues designing frameworks with atomic precision to address pressing energy and environmental challenges.

faculty.name: Jeffrey R. Long
faculty.title: C. Judson King Distinguished Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Long's group designs and synthesizes new inorganic materials and molecules to uncover and exploit novel physical phenomena. A major effort tailors metal-organic frameworks with coordinatively unsaturated metal sites for selective gas adsorption and separation, targeting carbon capture, oxygen and hydrocarbon purification, and hydrogen storage. Related work explores single-molecule magnets, spin-crossover behavior, and electrically conductive frameworks. By tuning metal-ligand interactions with atomic precision, the lab connects fundamental coordination chemistry to applications in energy, sustainable separations, and molecular magnetism.

faculty.name: John Arnold
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Arnold's group pursues synthetic inorganic and organometallic chemistry across the d-, p- and f-block, making unusual molecules and studying their structure and reactivity. Using tailored ligand frameworks, the lab prepares low-coordinate and low-valent complexes that activate small molecules such as dinitrogen, carbon dioxide, hydrogen and nitrous oxide, and that display novel catalytic behavior. A longstanding interest in actinide chemistry exploits the distinctive oxidation states and 5f orbitals of these elements to access bonding and reactivity unavailable elsewhere in the periodic table.

faculty.name: Felix R. Fischer
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Fischer's group develops bottom-up synthetic routes to atomically precise carbon nanomaterials, most notably graphene nanoribbons built from custom molecular precursors. By linking and cyclodehydrogenating designed monomers on surfaces, the lab controls ribbon width, edge structure and heteroatom doping to engineer electronic and magnetic properties emerging from quantum confinement and topology. Combining organic synthesis with scanning probe microscopy, they create nanoscale heterojunctions and quantum-engineered lattices, and collaborate with industry to integrate these materials into next-generation semiconductor devices.

faculty.name: Matthew B. Francis
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Francis's group invents new organic reactions for site-specific protein modification and uses them to build functional protein-based materials. The lab has developed highly selective bioconjugation chemistries, including oxidative couplings that target tyrosine and N-terminal residues, to attach cargo to proteins and viral capsids with precise control. These modified assemblies become platforms for diagnostic imaging agents, drug delivery, light-harvesting systems, and materials for water treatment, uniting synthetic methodology with applications that span medicine, energy, and the environment.

faculty.name: Alanna Schepartz
faculty.title: T.Z. and Irmgard Chu Distinguished Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Schepartz works across the chemistry-biology continuum to understand complex cellular processes and to design molecules with new functions. Her lab leads efforts to reprogram the ribosome and expand the genetic code, aiming to biosynthesize sequence-defined non-protein polymers through the NSF Center for Genetically Encoded Materials. Other projects develop cell-permeant molecular carriers that efficiently cross membranes and super-resolution probes for imaging organelle dynamics in living cells, connecting foundational chemistry to synthetic biology, biophysics, and cell biology.

faculty.name: Teresa Head-Gordon
faculty.title: Chancellor's Professor of Chemistry, Bioengineering, and Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering
faculty.summary: Head-Gordon develops theoretical and computational methods to model molecular liquids, biomolecules, and catalysis. Her group is known for advanced models of water and solvation and for multiscale simulations that bridge quantum mechanics and molecular dynamics. Current directions include machine-learning interatomic potentials that go beyond single-point energies, the surprising chemistry of accelerated reactions in aqueous microdroplets, and computational protein and drug design. By combining physical theory with data-driven approaches, the lab seeks predictive, transferable descriptions of complex chemical and biological systems.

faculty.name: K. Birgitta Whaley
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry; Director, Berkeley Quantum Information and Computation Center
faculty.summary: Whaley's theoretical group studies quantum information, quantum computation, and the control and simulation of complex quantum systems. Research spans quantum control and measurement, the analysis of open quantum systems and decoherence, macroscopic quantum states and quantum metrology, and algorithms for simulating chemistry on quantum computers. The group also probes possible quantum effects in biological processes such as energy and charge transfer. Directing Berkeley's quantum information center, Whaley connects fundamental quantum theory to emerging quantum technologies and coherent-science applications.

faculty.name: Naomi S. Ginsberg
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry and Physics
faculty.summary: Ginsberg's group develops new optical, electron, and X-ray imaging methods to spatially resolve fast dynamics in soft electronic and biological materials. By combining ultrafast spectroscopy, light microscopy, and cathodoluminescence electron microscopy, the lab visualizes how energy and charge move through heterogeneous systems, from photosynthetic light harvesting to semiconductor nanocrystals and organic semiconductors. Tracking these processes across many length and time scales reveals how nanoscale structure and disorder govern function, guiding the design of better light-harvesting and optoelectronic materials.

faculty.name: Stephen R. Leone
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry and Physics
faculty.summary: Leone's group uses ultrafast lasers and soft X-ray probes to watch electrons and nuclei move in real time. Pioneering attosecond and femtosecond transient-absorption methods, the lab captures the earliest steps of chemical and physical processes, tracking valence and core-level electronic dynamics in molecules, solids, and nanomaterials. Related work addresses state-resolved collision and reaction dynamics, aerosol and nanoparticle chemistry, and charge-carrier motion in semiconductors. These measurements reveal how energy flows during bond breaking, excitation, and charge transfer on natural timescales.

faculty.name: Evan R. Williams
faculty.title: Professor of Chemistry
faculty.summary: Williams develops and applies novel mass spectrometry, tandem mass spectrometry, and laser spectroscopy techniques to probe the structure and energetics of biomolecules and clusters. His group investigates protein and complex conformations in the gas phase, ion-water interactions and stepwise hydration, and the fundamental thermochemistry of charged species, connecting isolated-molecule measurements to behavior in solution. The lab also advances instrumentation and methods, including native mass spectrometry and ion mobility, and directs Berkeley's mass spectrometry facility supporting research across chemistry and biology.

programs.university: ETH Zürich (Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Zurich)

requirements.deadline_display: Rolling / no fixed deadline — doctoral positions are advertised year-round by individual professors
requirements.fee_display: Unknown/Verify (no central application fee; you apply to a professor's advertised position)
requirements.english: English is the working language of doctoral research; strong English proficiency is expected but there is no single fixed centralized test score (requirements set by the supervisor/ETH) — verify.
requirements.admission_model_note: Applicants must first secure a doctoral position by contacting and being accepted by an ETH professor; formal matriculation follows only after a supervisor agrees to take the student. There are no first-year rotations.

funding.note: Doctoral researchers are employed and salaried by ETH as research/teaching assistants (among the highest PhD salaries in Europe); positions typically run about 3-4 years and include teaching or service duties.

requirements.contact_note: Contacting a professor is effectively required — you must be accepted into a research group before you can register for the doctorate.

programs.data_currency: Researched 2026-07-05 from official pages — verify before applying.

faculty.name: Erick M. Carreira
faculty.title: Full Professor of Organic Chemistry
faculty.summary: Carreira's group conducts research in complex natural-product total synthesis and the development of catalytic asymmetric methods. The group is known for iridium-catalyzed enantioselective allylic substitutions and other stereoselective transformations, which it applies to concise syntheses of intricate, biologically active targets. Recent work spans new catalytic reactions, medicinal-chemistry collaborations, and molecules that probe biological function, combining inventive strategy with mechanistic understanding to access architecturally demanding compounds and to establish broadly useful, selective synthetic methodology.

faculty.name: Bill Morandi
faculty.title: Full Professor of Organic Chemistry
faculty.summary: Morandi develops new catalytic reactions that improve the efficiency and sustainability of organic synthesis. The group pioneered shuttle catalysis and isodesmic strategies that transfer functional groups reversibly between molecules, enabling reactions such as retro-hydroformylation and the interconversion of nitriles and alkenes. Recent work advances metal-catalyzed C-H and C-C functionalization, single-atom and skeletal editing, and mechanistic tools for reaction discovery. By reimagining how bonds are made and broken, the group targets greener routes and previously inaccessible disconnections.

faculty.name: Helma Wennemers
faculty.title: Full Professor of Organic Chemistry
faculty.summary: Wennemers combines organic chemistry and chemical biology to design functional peptides. Her group develops short peptide catalysts that achieve high stereoselectivity in aldol and conjugate-addition reactions, and studies collagen model peptides to understand and program the stability and assembly of this key structural protein. Recent work also creates peptide-based scaffolds for controlled delivery, tumor targeting, and templated synthesis of metal nanoparticles. By linking sequence to function, the group builds tunable molecular systems bridging catalysis, materials, and biomedical applications.

faculty.name: Jeffrey W. Bode
faculty.title: Full Professor of Organic Chemistry
faculty.summary: Bode develops synthetic methods and reagents for making and modifying complex molecules, with an emphasis on chemoselective ligations. His group invented the KAHA (alpha-ketoacid-hydroxylamine) ligation for peptide and protein synthesis and SnAP reagents for saturated N-heterocycles, and builds molecular tools for bioconjugation and diversity synthesis. Recent work spans synthetic peptides and proteins, functional materials, and platforms for rapid reaction discovery and screening, aiming to make challenging chemical structures reliably accessible for chemical biology and pharmaceutical research.

faculty.name: Christophe Copéret
faculty.title: Full Professor of Surface and Interfacial Chemistry
faculty.summary: Copéret bridges molecular and materials chemistry through surface organometallic chemistry, building well-defined single-site catalysts on oxide supports to connect structure with catalytic function. The group uses advanced solid-state NMR, including dynamic nuclear polarization, and spectroscopy to characterize active sites and mechanisms. Recent work targets CO2 hydrogenation to methanol, alkane and olefin conversion, and tailored materials for energy and sustainability, alongside fundamental studies of bonding at interfaces, uniting precise synthesis with deep spectroscopic and mechanistic understanding of catalysis.

faculty.name: Maksym Kovalenko
faculty.title: Full Professor of Inorganic Chemistry; Head of the Laboratory of Inorganic Chemistry
faculty.summary: Kovalenko's group synthesizes and studies inorganic nanomaterials, especially colloidal semiconductor nanocrystals and lead-halide perovskite quantum dots. The group develops precise, scalable syntheses and surface chemistries and explores how size, composition, and assembly control light emission and charge transport. Recent work advances perovskite nanocrystals for displays, LEDs, single-photon sources, and quantum-information applications, as well as materials for batteries and photodetectors. Jointly appointed with Empa, the group links fundamental nanochemistry to optoelectronic and energy technologies.

faculty.name: Victor Mougel
faculty.title: Assistant Professor (Tenure Track) of Inorganic Chemistry
faculty.summary: Mougel designs bioinspired molecules and materials for sustainable catalysis, drawing on the structures of natural metalloenzymes. His group builds synthetic iron-sulfur clusters and molecular catalysts to drive small-molecule transformations such as CO2 reduction, nitrogen and nitrate conversion, and water oxidation, and integrates them into electrodes and materials for renewable-energy applications. Recent work, recognized by the Ruzicka Prize, elucidates how cluster structure controls redox behavior and catalysis, connecting fundamental bioinorganic chemistry to practical electrocatalytic and environmental technologies.

faculty.name: Sereina Riniker
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Computational Chemistry
faculty.summary: Riniker develops computational methods to model molecules in solution and to predict their properties. Her group advances classical and machine-learned force fields, enhanced-sampling and free-energy techniques, and cheminformatics approaches such as molecular dynamics fingerprints. Recent work couples physics-based simulation with machine learning to improve accuracy and speed for drug-discovery-relevant conformational sampling and binding predictions, and studies reactive and condensed-phase systems. By connecting molecular dynamics with data-driven models, the group builds tools that make simulation more predictive and broadly applicable.

faculty.name: Renato Zenobi
faculty.title: Full Professor of Analytical Chemistry
faculty.summary: Zenobi develops analytical and bioanalytical chemistry, particularly advanced mass spectrometry and nanoscale spectroscopy. His group creates methods for ambient ionization, breath and exhaled-aerosol analysis for diagnostics, single-cell and spatially resolved metabolomics, and studies of noncovalent complexes. The group also pioneers tip-enhanced Raman spectroscopy and near-field techniques that push chemical imaging toward the nanometer scale. Recent work applies these tools to biomedical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and fundamental measurement science, advancing the sensitivity, resolution, and reach of chemical analysis.

faculty.name: Javier Pérez-Ramírez
faculty.title: Full Professor of Catalysis Engineering
faculty.summary: Pérez-Ramírez leads research in catalysis engineering aimed at carbon-neutral and resource-efficient chemical manufacturing. His group designs and scales heterogeneous catalysts, including single-atom and zeolite materials, for CO2 and CO valorization, methane and alkane activation, plastics recycling, and halogen chemistry, integrating catalyst discovery with reaction engineering and life-cycle thinking. As director of NCCR Catalysis, the group pursues sustainable routes across the value chain, combining high-throughput experimentation, mechanistic study, and process design to translate fundamental catalysis into greener industrial technologies.
